text: Exposure low in the right chest can be challenging, especially with a high-riding diaphragm.
Posterior pleura is opened along the length of the azagus vein with an energy device, ligasure in this case.
The azagus vein arch is divided with a vascular stapler.
Large intercostal veins can be encountered and should be carefully clipped as they can retract and cause bleeding that obscures the clarity of the field.
We use penrose drains in order to retract the esophagus, and this greatly facilitates dissection.

While completing the dissection of the esophagus of the pericardium, it is important to be cautious about the pulmonary vein which can be stretched and tethered into the dissection field with distraction.
This can be injured as demonstrated here.

Standard techniques to management of vascular injury are applied in this scenario, including arresting the bleeding with a sponge stick, compression, prompt thoracotomy and exploration, and overswing the vein with proline sutures.
Cautious and limited use of energy near the airway is critical to avoid delayed fistulization.
In addition, avoiding overinflation of the double lumen balloon decreases risk of injury.
laparoscopic port access as demonstrated here and utilize a Nathanson liver retractor.
We start the intra-abdominal dissection by division of the pars flaccida.
For malignant disease, dissection is completed close to the liver.
The right gastric artery is preserved to optimize the conduit health.
It is important to look for a replaced or accessory left hepatic artery within the pars flaccida.
Dissection of the left gastric pedicle is completed by skeletonizing the vessel circumferentially while sweeping lymph nodes into the specimen.
Division is completed with a vascular stapler.
If a D2 dissection is completed,
the hepatic and splenic arteries escalidinize for lymph node removal.
In this case, it is important to be cautious about the portal vein and IVC that are at risk for injury.
Dissection of the greater curve of the stomach needs to proceed cautiously in order to protect the right gastroepiploic artery.
This is especially true near the gastric outlet as shown here, where the artery tends to drift away from the stomach in addition to the omentum thickening and left colon enroaching into the space.
The lesser omental sac is entered at least 2 cm away from the gastroepiploic artery and an omental flap can be fashioned to wrap the stable line of the conduit or anastomosis.
Entering the lesser omentum is more challenging in obese patients with a fused omentum.
In this case, cautiously proceeding to ensure that the transverse colon is protected and avoiding accidental entry into the mesocolon
Using a no-touch technique while handling the gastric conduit optimizes the health of the conduit and decreases risk of gastro-epiploic artery injury.
There are several options to performing a pyloric drainage with our preference being injection of 100 units of Botox.
This is injected at the pylorus sphincter and its pharmacological duration is estimated at 90 to 120 days.
Construction of the conduit is started by stapling just proximal to the right gastric bundle or crow's feet.
Once again, during construction, it is critical to minimize handling the conduit.
However, it may be grasped at the terminal end, which will be resected eventually.
We aim for a 3.5 centimeter conduit to optimize drainage and mark this in order to guide our stapling.
Multiple staple loads are required, ensuring that the staple line is kept parallel to the curve of the greater curve of the stomach in order to optimize conduit length.
Avoidance of staple shelves and notches between staple loads may decrease the risk of staple line leak.
Between staple fires, it is important to ensure that the conduit is not spiraling.
Once constructed, we suture the conduit and the specimen together.
We also mark it to ensure correct orientation during delivery into the neck.
We have at times used laser-assisted endocyanine green fluorescent dye angiography via the spy system in order to evaluate the perfusion of our conduit.
This remains a promising modality that is currently under further investigation.
During delivery of the conduit into the neck, gentle traction
is provided in the neck and concurrent assistance is provided laparoscopically to ensure correct orientation.
It is important not to deliver excess conduit.
Tacking the conduit to the diaphragm or the cura may help prevent future paraconduit hernias, which have a higher incidence in minimally invasive esophagectomies.
A feeding jejunostomy is inserted 20 centimeters distal to the ligament of trites.
A purse string suture is inserted around the aperture of the tube and four quadrant sutures via T fasteners are inserted in order to tack the bowel to the abdominal wall.
A Sendinger type jejunostomy tube is inserted.
Further distal and proximal tacking sutures to the abdominal wall are also inserted in order to decrease the risk of torsion.
